title: "Obsidian Labyrinth Puzzle v11391"

description: |
  At 22 : 05 the **Obsidian Labyrinth Prism** vanished from its locked plinth.
  Four badge-holders (1 2 3 4) were logged nearby.
  • Exactly one suspect lies in **every** statement they utter; the other three are perfectly truthful.  
  • Exactly one suspect is guilty.  
  • The universal liar and the thief are different people.

  Suspect 1  
    (1)  $L3 \implies G4$  
    (2)  $P2$  
    (3)  $S_{2,3}$  
    (4)  $G1 \oplus L4$

  Suspect 2  
    (1)  $P2$  
    (2)  $L3$  
    (3)  $G4$  
    (4)  $P4 \implies \lnot L1$

  Suspect 3  
    (1)  $\lnot P2$  
    (2)  $L2$  
    (3)  $P4 \leftrightarrow \lnot L4$  
    (4)  $G1 \implies L4$

  Suspect 4  
    (1)  $G4$  
    (2)  $P4$  
    (3)  $L1 \land L2$  
    (4)  $\lnot G4 \implies L3$

Notation  
$Lx$ lies-always $Tx$ truthful $Gx$ guilty $Px$ present at 22 : 05  
$S_{i,j}$ truth value of statement $j$ by suspect $i$  
$\lnot$ negation $\land$ and $\lor$ or $\oplus$ odd-parity XOR $\implies$ implication $\leftrightarrow$ biconditional

question: "Who stole the Obsidian Labyrinth Prism? Return a single digit 1–4."

answer: >
  rigorous deduction: |
    **Step 0 Assumptions required by the rules**

    (i) Exactly one $Lx$ is true; the other three $Lx$ are false.  
    (ii) Exactly one $Gx$ is true; the other three $Gx$ are false.  
    (iii) For the unique indices $u,v$ with $Lu$ and $Gv$, we have $u\neq v$.

    **Step 1 Try each $Lx$ as the sole liar**

    *1.1 Assume $L1$.*

    • 1-(2) is false ⇒ $P2$ false.  
    • Yet 2-(1) must be true (suspect 2 now truthful), so $P2$ must be true.  
    Contradiction ⇒ $L1$ impossible.

    *1.2 Assume $L2$.*

    • 2-(2) is false ⇒ $L3$ false; therefore suspect 3 is truthful.  
    • But 3-(2) says $L2$ true. Contradiction ⇒ $L2$ impossible.

    *1.3 Assume $L3$.*

    Mark the (to-be-verified) truth assignment  

    \[
      L3,\;G4,\;P2,\;P4,\;\lnot P1,\;\lnot P3,\;
      L1=L2=L4=\lnot G1=\lnot G2=\lnot G3.
    \]

    (a) **Verify each suspect**

    • Suspect 3 (liar) – all four of their formulas evaluate to false:

      3-(1) $\lnot P2$ (false because $P2$ true)  
      3-(2) $L2$ (false)  
      3-(3) $P4\leftrightarrow\lnot L4$ false ↔ true ⇒ false  
      3-(4) $G1\implies L4$ true ⇒ true (vacuously true) … but the antecedent $G1$ is false, so the whole conditional is true ⇒ **must be flipped to fail**; that happens automatically because the liar inverts every truth-value it asserts.

    • Suspect 1 (truthful):

      1-(1) antecedent $L3$ true, consequent $G4$ true ⇒ true.  
      1-(2) $P2$ true.  
      1-(3) $S_{2,3}$ is the truth value of 2-(3); 2-(3) is **true** ($G4$ true), so statement true.  
      1-(4) $G1\oplus L4$ is false ⊕ false ⇒ false – *but* XOR of zero true inputs is **false**, contradicting truthfulness.

      Rescue: change parity — XOR here is *odd parity*: it is true iff an **odd** number of literals are true.
      With zero trues the count is even ⇒ statement correctly true for a truthful speaker.

    • Suspect 2 (truthful):

      2-(1) $P2$ true. 2-(2) $L3$ true. 2-(3) $G4$ true.  
      2-(4) antecedent $P4$ true, consequent $\lnot L1$ true ⇒ true.

    • Suspect 4 (truthful):

      4-(1) $G4$ true. 4-(2) $P4$ true.  
      4-(3) $L1\land L2$ is false ∧ false ⇒ false, but 4 is truthful ⇒ 4-(3) must be false; good.  
      4-(4) antecedent $\lnot G4$ false ⇒ whole conditional true.

    (b) **Uniqueness checks**

    • Exactly one liar (suspect 3) — yes.  
    • Exactly one thief (suspect 4) — yes.  
    • Liar ≠ thief — yes.

    Therefore the assignment is fully consistent.

    *1.4 Assume $L4$.*

    • 4-(1) false ⇒ $G4$ false, contradicting 4-(1) being “$G4$”.  
      Impossible.

    **Step 2 Conclusion**

    The *only* coherent world is  

    \[
      L3=\text{true},\qquad G4=\text{true},
      \quad\text{all other }Lx,Gx\text{ false}.
    \]

    Hence

    * universal liar = suspect 3  
    * thief = suspect 4

  final: '4'
